item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the other commenters. This has a very interesting idea, but I feel like it could be expanded upon. It has so much unused potential.
That said, I enjoyed it as is enough to give a +1.

item.title: Re: Image Source
item.content:encoded:
Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Periodic-table.jpg
Author: LeVanHan
License: CC BY-SA 3.0, 2.5, 2.0, 1.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I enjoy things related to chemical elements and compounds, I also have to no-vote this. I agree with lynx and Americium that the explanation of the elements not conforming to standard electron models is not satisfying.

Here's the thing; you wrote this object that has the potential to pretty much be nerd porn for anyone with more than a passing interest in chemistry, but it takes a wonderful idea and doesn't take it to the next level. If you were willing to really dig into the physics of the behavior of NEW ELEMENTS, you'd be surprised how receptive everybody would be to that level of detail.

I also have to second concerns about the creation of unstable elements. I'm sure you know how unstable many of the artificially replicated elements on OUR periodic table are. As in, only existing for fractions of a second. They are they nerd saying 'well, technically' and pushing its glasses up, of the chemistry world. The point is, to even detect and measure those, chemists have to be actively observing with insanely powerful equipment. I would like it if you explained what method is used by SCP researchers to detect these anomalous unstable elements, since I'm assuming they must be always-on, passive detection methods, since they don't know when they'll show up.

All that being said, this is solid conceptually, and well written. I'd just like to see more science behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The memetic effect is pretty boring and something that I feel has been done already quite a few times. I didn't really find this too interesting.

That being said, it certainly wasn't bad, and the concept was nice. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not really - if they're stable, they already behave according to laws of chemistry. x3

Not necessarily- there are actually a few hypothetical "novel" particles that some theorists have put together that could create atoms if physics worked slightly differently- if squarks, for instance, existed, they could be used interchangeably with quarks for building bizzaro-world versions of neutrons, electrons, and protons- and thus atoms with bizarre properties: say, hydrogen-like atoms with the mass of a lead atom, or magnetic carbon, or elements that react to gravitational fields like normal elements do to electrical fields, and vice versa.

And thats before you get into the whole "what if there were additional fundamental forces" and you get magictons that interact with aethertrons in the same way as protons and electrons. That'd give you things like atoms made of 1 proton, 1 magicton, and 2 neutrons that react like hydrogen but otherwise have the properties of helium, and magitron hydrogen that doesn't react with proton hydrogen, but does react with other magictron hydrogen atoms… it just gets weirder from there.

My take on this thing is that its demonstrating what chemistry looks like in an extremely alternate universe(s) by providing examples, possibly as some sort of misguided attempt to make friendly scientific contact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'm glad you like it. I can see why you might think of it as Keter, and I suppose there is some gray area there. Personally, I thought of it as Euclid both because it's pretty well contained at this point and it's pretty slow. The radiation levels are rising, yes, but if it wasn't contained at all, there would be plenty of time to re-contain it before it ruined the world. I decided to err on the side of Euclid, since I've seen SCP articles with addenda that say "reclassification to Keter denied/accepted," suggesting that some arguably Keter SCPs at least start out as Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'm glad you like it, and also, I am thinking of ways I could expand upon it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always been interested in the periodic table (and the elements in particular) so this is a really fascinating SCP for me. I also, like TL333s, enjoyed the idea of the Foundation having an expanded periodic table.

Although, considering that this thing could cause an end-of-the-world scenario if containment slipped up, and considering the radiation levels around it are still rising, wouldn't it be qualified as Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept of this scip. I think it would be pretty cool if (eventually) this could be linked up with some sort of Tale, incident, or experimentation log. Still giving it a +1 for current content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Replace it with something clever and credible, and if you canot find something like that, just erase it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of an "expanded" periodic table as well, but don't give me the credit. The first place I ever saw the phrase mentioned was in SCP-2004. I don't know who came up with it originally. Anyway, I'm glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The memetic effect is pretty standard, but I like the transmutation idea and I really like that the Foundation has an "expanded" periodic table.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'm glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, well I'm glad we could clear that up. I'll edit the article in short order.

As for the anomalous elements damaging the laws of chemistry, eh, I suppose this gets into a philosophical conundrum. When people write from the POV of other GOIs, one of the main criticisms leveled at the SCP Foundation is their commitment to enforcing "consensus reality." Perhaps the Foundation believes that because these elements do not conform to the established scientific model of chemistry, they are anomalous (that is kind of what the word means, after all) and thus potentially harmful. I think I'll let the readers decide for themselves on this score.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what can be done to improve the memetic effect, really, but as for the elements decaying, keep in mind that most of the anomalous elements decay, and that's good, because radiation is easy to deal with. The ones that don't are the problem, seeing as how they're distorting the laws of physics just to keep existing.

I imagine that they figured out that anomalous elements were being produced by testing samples procured at containment. When a composition test turns up an unusually high percentage of inconclusive results, that's a hint. There's also the idea that an organization that regularly consults an "expanded periodic table" may have more esoteric means of detecting anomalous elements.

Thanks for the link, but I am aware of the "island of stability" concept. If I were to expand the periodic table solely by adding transuranic elements, then, to me, it would just make too much sense. Adding elements to the left, right, and above the periodic table makes no sense whatsoever, and I believe this serves to add a horrifying absurdity, the idea that the multiverse isn't half as reasonable as we imagine.

Overall, I understand your point of view, but I feel that the reasons you listed are better left as is. I thought you might like an explanation, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, CPM trumps Bq and whatever else in this case, and it was something of a derp on my side, and the above isn't a measure I'd object to.

That said, power incident does have its uses as most dosimetric measurements are based on received energy from radiation - therefore all it takes is assume reasonable area for a human body, and work out how high would it have to be to deposit a dangerous dose over a reasonable time someone is expected to be in the chamber.

also because that irradiated air contains stable anomalous elements, whose very existence may damage the laws of chemistry.

Not really - if they're stable, they already behave according to laws of chemistry. x3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you mean with that first sentence. I'll look into revising it.

Now as for the redaction, I can understand people being bored of this sort of thing. Do you think it would be better if I were to delete that "[DATA EXPUNGED]," or try and replace it with something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the input, but I just spent the past half hour trying to figure out dangerous levels of radiation in W/m2 and I'm stumped.

Based on my research, I understand now that rem is inappropriate for measuring the radioactivity of the air in a room, but Bq seems to be an absolute value that one would only be able to reliably measure if the sample were completely enclosed in a container.

For this containment chamber, I envision it being equipped with multiple radiation sensors, in which case CPM would be an appropriate measurement. The advantage of using CPM is that it's simple, not just for my sake but for the containment MTF guys who are called in after a breach and all the scientists are dead.

Based on this source-

http://modernsurvivalblog.com/nuclear/radiation-geiger-counter-the-radiation-network/

-I'm going to go with 2,000 CPM. I chose this figure conservatively for two reasons: one, because the idea is to not let radiation build up to the point where it becomes a serious health hazard in a building where people work and/or live, and also because that irradiated air contains stable anomalous elements, whose very existence may damage the laws of chemistry. Naturally, the Foundation doesn't want those building up to an appreciable concentration.

I feel that this is the most reasonable figure that I, personally, could come up with, but if you can recommend a better figure, by all means do so. Again, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good. Well done, KingGolem. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's actually a better idea. Listen to the lynx.

And as far as the actual number goes, I truly have no idea what to suggest, since it's anomalous. Sorry. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am… disappointed.

The picture is really neat, and gave me somewhat high hopes for the article - what can I say, chemistry going to hell in a handbasket is a fun idea to play with.

However, what it ends up being is a not exactly interesting memetic effect, and, hmm. While I can't exactly argue with the logic of, well, having the new elements decay immediately (which is just what'd happen) , it's sort of a boring way out, and leads to internal logic problems.
Specifically, how did they ever find out this is what happens? The new elements decay in negligible time because their composition is full of shit, and you can't even derive the hypothesis from reaction byproducts, because it deposits random elements out of a huge set. Besides, this doesn't even say what amounts of them are deposited…. Ultimately this reminds me of SCORPION STARE from Charlie Stross's books, except that one worked as a "scientific" explanation of the mythic medusae turning people and animals and whatevs into stone, while this has nothing particularly interesting going on.

As far as potential ways of improving this go, I leave you with this wikipedia article. Maybe it's right, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd suggest using something like CPM (counts per minute) if you're going to keep that sentence the way it is.

The actual proper unit is Becquerel (Bq) giving one nucleus decaying per second.

However, estimating the room's enviroment… I'd say it might be best to work with incident radiation in W/m2 aka power incident on area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I want to state that this is an increidbly good article for someone who has been here for barely a day, so I'll give it that to you. Sadly, it's not good enough for me to upvote bud.

Some of these anomalous elements are listed on the Foundation’s expanded periodic table, but most are not.

Human beings exposed to this effect will begin to show symptoms similar to heavy metal poisoning, radiation sickness, and [DATA EXPUNGED].

The first sentence bugs me for some reason. Mostly because it reminds me the usual newbie's grammar. As for the second, redacting the last (and possibly most interesting side effect of this thing) is quite boring and overused. And you've done it several times throughout the article, so I'll better give a no-vote.

Now, for the good side, it's a simple idea that doesn't need a super complicated story behind to work by itself, and it's rather weird to see those nowadays1.

Footnotes
1. Most of them go down pretty hard and get their metaphorical asses baleeted after 24 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Yeah, for that picture I was going for a "so silly it's terrifying" kind of vibe. I'm glad you like it, and tell your fiancee I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw the picture and exclaimed, "Ooooooh!!!" and woke up my fiancee. -1!!!!

Just kidding. I love this. Very nice work. This is just the sort of thing that makes my imagination light up and obsess for a bit. Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I was inspired after reading SCP-2004, which is the first I recall hearing of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, thank you for your critique.

First of all, thank you for pointing out that I used the wrong unit. I'm getting the impression that you know more about nuclear physics than me. How many CPM do you think would be dangerous to have floating around in a room? Or, alternatively, how could I re-write the sentence to accomodate some other measurement? The whole purpose of cycling the air is twofold: one, so it doesn't pose a health risk to the Site or the D-class personnel, and two, because these are anomalous elements, and it's probably a bad idea to let them build up anyway.

The reason the air is pumped out, canned, and stored away is because it's not just radioactive, it has atoms and isotopes of anomalous elements in it, whose properties cannot be predicted. Some of these atoms rapidly decompose, but some are stable. As such, the current containment procedure is to keep the elements from building up to a high concentration.

Lastly, though I cannot argue with your neutral vote, I will say that I chose to explicitly point out that the anomalous elements do not conform to standard electron configuration rules because I didn't think extrapolating on the periodic table in a logical fashion would be as interesting. If I wanted to extrapolate logically, I'd only put elements on the end, and use the "islands of stability" thing as a justification. What I wanted was strictly illogical elements, since we are dealing with other universes. I recall reading once that Mendeleev believed there was an element lighter than hydrogen, which he called coronium. That makes no sense whatsoever, and everyone knows it, but that's just the sort of thing that this SCP deal with.

I hope that cleared things up, and again, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes sense, though I was far more a fan of "this is all the shit we haven't discovered yet" rather than "whoops conveniently placed alternate universe".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the reference to "the Foundation's expanded periodic table". Rest of it is good too. Good first try!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, it makes them believe that the anomalous periodic table is the correct one for this universe. SCP-2046 is specifically drawing elements from other universes that can't exist in our own. It's also drawing in the "concept" of the periodic table from other universes, which over-writes their own understanding of the periodic table.

I hope that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It may be that only staff can create new threads in the announcements forums, but any user can reply to an existing thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The one issue I have is the the part where it makes people believe the new elements exist. Of course they believe! The new elements are sitting right there in front of them!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't required to announce new things, but people do post in that forum. I've never thought it was staff only, so you may be looking at the wrong one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed. Thank you for pointing that out.

Also, the "How to write an SCP" guide says to announce it in the announcements forum, but that forum is admin only. Do you know what I'm supposed to do?

Nevermind, I figured it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fix the title format (capitalize it) and I'll edit this post later, after I've voted and read it.

Edit: Particular thing: (You know you should've been prepared for this, based on my username :P)

Should the radiation inside the containment chamber begin to exceed 50 rem, the air must be evacuated, stored in shielded gas canisters, and disposed of.

REM stands for Roentgen Equivalent Man, and it's a measurement of dose rather than the amount of radiation in a room. Dose = particular to the organism exposed/contaminated, whereas the amount of radiation in a room is independent of the organism involved. I'd suggest using something like CPM (counts per minute) if you're going to keep that sentence the way it is.

Also, I don't know why you'd have to 'dispose of' the air in there if it's just going to get contaminated again. You could just ventilate it in a closed duct circuit. Now, are you talking about contamination, or are you talking about exposure? Does whatever is emitted from this actually get on (contaminate) stuff in the room, in which case, it would be radioactively contaminated particles rather than just rays and particles (I'm sorry for the science jargon. There's no other way to talk about the nuclear field) or is the room just exposed to radiation and does not become radioactive?

As far as the skip goes, it's not badly written at all, but I am neutral-voting on account that I feel like you just tacked on the fact that it doesn't conform to normal electron configuration rules/etc. just so that you wouldn't have to deal with extrapolating upon the periodic table in a logical manner.

Edit 2: Here is the best image I could find that illustrates the difference between exposure and contamination. The beest 1 evar is on my phone app, but I couldn't find the GIF online. :(

item.title: Image Source
item.content:encoded:
Hey everyone, this is my first SCP, and I'm not really sure what this image falls under. I took a generic image of a periodic table from this source: http://www.middlebury.net/op-ed/periodic-table.html and modified it to its current form. I don't know if that needs an attribution or anything, but Google image search seems to show it on more than one site, so I think it's okay.

By all means, somebody let me know if I messed up, with the image or otherwise.
